intro: Eating well during pregnancy supports your baby's growth and helps you feel your best. The ideas below are general, Indian-friendly suggestions for a balanced diet. They are educational only and are not a substitute for personal medical advice — please discuss your individual dietary needs, supplements, and any health conditions with your own healthcare provider.
proteinSources: Dal and lentils (toor, moong, masoor, chana)
Chicken and fish (well cooked, low-mercury varieties)
Sprouts and legumes such as chana and rajma
ironRich: Spinach and other leafy greens (palak, methi)
Pair iron foods with vitamin C (lemon, amla, citrus) to help absorption
mealIdeas: A balanced thali with dal, sabzi, roti or rice, curd, and a small salad
Vegetable poha or upma with a glass of milk
Idli or dosa with sambar and a side of chutney
Roti with paneer or egg bhurji and a vegetable
Ragi porridge or ragi malt with jaggery for an iron and calcium boost
Khichdi with vegetables, topped with a spoon of ghee and a side of curd
Fruit and nut snack with a handful of soaked almonds and dates
hydration: Aim for regular sips of clean water through the day
Include buttermilk (chaas) and coconut water for variety and electrolytes
Fresh fruit and vegetable soups add fluids and nutrients
Limit very sugary drinks and packaged juices
Increase fluids in hot weather and if you feel thirsty or tired
avoidOrLimit: Raw or undercooked meat, eggs, and fish
Unpasteurised milk and soft unpasteurised cheeses
High-mercury fish such as shark, swordfish, and king mackerel
Excess caffeine from coffee, tea, and cola (keep intake modest)
Alcohol, which is best avoided entirely in pregnancy
Unwashed fruits and vegetables, and street food of uncertain hygiene
Papaya (especially raw or unripe) and pineapple are traditionally limited by some families; evidence in normal food amounts is limited, so follow your own provider's guidance
Very salty, deep-fried, and heavily processed foods in large amounts
